presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: Sudden sharp chest pain on the left side
I suddenly couldn't breathe properly and have chest pain
Sharp pain on one side of my chest that came out of nowhere
Chest pain and breathlessness that started out of nowhere while I was sitting
Breathing became painful and difficult all of a sudden

history_data.hpi: Patient is a 24-year-old tall, thin male who developed sudden onset sharp left-sided chest pain approximately one hour ago while sitting at his desk. The pain began without any precipitant and was immediately followed by progressive breathlessness. Pain is pleuritic — worse on deep inspiration and coughing. No preceding exertion, cough, trauma, or respiratory illness. No fever or productive cough. Patient is a current smoker. He has never had this before but recalls being told he had a 'tall, thin chest' at a previous health check. He is visibly anxious and in pain.
patient_quotes: The pain came completely out of nowhere — I was just sitting at my desk.
It's a sharp pain on the left side that gets worse when I breathe in.
I suddenly felt like I couldn't take a full breath.
I've never had anything like this before.
I was completely fine and then it just happened.
pmh_pool: Current smoker — 10 cigarettes per day for 5 years
Tall, thin body habitus — marfanoid features noted at previous GP visit

social_history.smoking: Current smoker — 10 cigarettes per day for 5 years (approximately 2.5 pack-years)
social_history.exercise: Moderately active — gym twice per week

negative_history: No recent trauma or chest injury
No recent surgery or invasive procedure
No leg swelling or calf pain
No family history of pneumothorax or Marfan syndrome

general_appearance: Tall and slim — body habitus consistent with risk phenotype
Splinting — guarding left side of chest to minimise respiratory movement
cardiovascular: Trachea midline — no deviation (no tension pneumothorax)
respiratory: Markedly reduced breath sounds — left hemithorax
Right lung clear — normal breath sounds throughout

investigations.Chest X-Ray: Large left-sided pneumothorax — visible pleural line with complete absence of peripheral lung markings in the left hemithorax. Estimated >2cm rim of air at hilum level — consistent with large pneumothorax by BTS criteria. No mediastinal shift. No right-sided pneumothorax. No pleural effusion. Trachea remains midline — no features of tension pneumothorax on this film.
investigations.ABG (Arterial Blood Gas): pH 7.44, PaO2 72 mmHg (hypoxaemia on air), PaCO2 33 mmHg (mild hyperventilation), HCO3 22 mmol/L, SpO2 92% on air. Acute respiratory alkalosis with hypoxaemia consistent with significant pneumothorax. Supplemental oxygen indicated.
investigations.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 112 bpm. No acute ST changes. No right heart strain pattern. Left-sided QRS voltage reduction may be noted — consistent with ipsilateral pneumothorax. No features of PE or ACS.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 9.2 × 10⁹/L (normal — no infection), Hb 14.8 g/dL, Platelets 284 × 10⁹/L. Normal haematological parameters — no evidence of underlying haematological abnormality.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, Urea 5.2 mmol/L — all normal. Useful baseline prior to any procedural intervention.
investigations.D-Dimer: 320 ng/mL — mildly elevated, likely due to physiological stress response. Pulmonary embolism remains a differential; however, clinical picture, young age, and CXR findings are more consistent with pneumothorax. CT-PA not indicated at this stage.

patient_rules.emotional_state: anxious and in pain — frightened by the sudden onset, trying to take shallow breaths to minimise pain

essential_questions: exact time of onset and what patient was doing at the time
character of chest pain — sharp, pleuritic, sudden onset
pain variation with breathing and coughing
any preceding trauma, exertion, or invasive procedure
smoking history — major risk factor for primary spontaneous pneumothorax
known lung disease — COPD, asthma, CF, sarcoidosis (secondary spontaneous pneumothorax)
body habitus — tall and thin body type is a recognised risk factor
connective tissue disease — Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
fever or productive cough — to exclude pneumonia
leg swelling or calf pain — to exclude PE
essential_examinations: general assessment — body habitus, respiratory distress, splinting
tracheal position — deviation away from affected side indicates tension pneumothorax
auscultation — absent or markedly reduced breath sounds over affected side
chest expansion — reduced on affected side
heart rate and blood pressure — haemodynamic compromise suggests tension
jugular venous pressure — raised JVP with hypotension suggests tension pneumothorax
critical_actions: immediately assess for tension pneumothorax — tracheal deviation, raised JVP, haemodynamic collapse, absent breath sounds: if present, needle decompression BEFORE chest X-ray
obtain chest X-ray to confirm pneumothorax and estimate size if patient is stable
classify as primary (no underlying lung disease) vs secondary (known lung disease) — management differs significantly
size classification by BTS criteria: small = <2cm rim of air at hilum; large = ≥2cm rim
large primary spontaneous pneumothorax: aspirate with 16–18G cannula in 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line — up to 2.5L; if fails, chest drain insertion
small primary spontaneous pneumothorax with minimal symptoms: may discharge with 1-week outpatient follow-up and safety-netting advice
high-flow oxygen 10–15L/min via non-rebreather mask — accelerates reabsorption of pleural air
secondary spontaneous pneumothorax: admit ALL patients regardless of size; lower threshold for chest drain
repeat chest X-ray after aspiration or drain insertion to confirm lung re-expansion
smoking cessation counselling — reduces recurrence risk
refer to thoracics for pleurodesis or VATS after second ipsilateral pneumothorax
advise patient: no flying for 1 week after full radiological resolution; no scuba diving ever unless surgical pleurodesis performed
red_flags: Tracheal deviation away from affected side — tension pneumothorax, immediate needle decompression required
Hypotension with absent breath sounds — tension pneumothorax, do not wait for CXR
Raised JVP with haemodynamic instability — tension physiology
SpO2 <92% on air — significant respiratory compromise
Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax in patient with underlying lung disease — higher mortality, admit all
Failure of lung to re-expand after aspiration — chest drain required
acceptable_differentials: Pulmonary embolism — pleuritic pain and dyspnoea, but CXR typically normal; D-dimer mildly elevated here but clinical picture favours pneumothorax
Acute coronary syndrome — central crushing pain, not pleuritic; ECG and troponin would differentiate
Acute pericarditis — pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea, but positional (relieved leaning forward), no unilateral signs
Musculoskeletal chest wall pain — no dyspnoea, reproducible on palpation, no CXR findings
Pneumonia — fever, productive cough, crackles rather than absent breath sounds; dullness not hyperresonance on percussion
management_actions: Immediate tracheal position assessment — exclude tension pneumothorax
Chest X-Ray — confirm diagnosis and assess size
ABG on air — assess degree of hypoxaemia
Large pneumothorax (≥2cm): needle aspiration — 16–18G cannula, 2nd ICS midclavicular line, aspirate up to 2.5L
If aspiration fails: chest drain insertion — Seldinger technique, 4th–5th ICS, anterior axillary line
Repeat CXR after intervention to confirm re-expansion
Analgesia: paracetamol and ibuprofen; opiates if severe
Smoking cessation counselling — strongly advised; smoking increases recurrence risk 12-fold
If small pneumothorax and minimal symptoms: discharge with safety-netting and outpatient follow-up in 1 week
Advise no flying until 1 week after confirmed radiological resolution
Advise no scuba diving unless bilateral surgical pleurodesis has been performed
Refer to thoracic surgery after second ipsilateral episode for consideration of pleurodesis or VATS bullectomy
critical_history_questions: What exactly were you doing when the pain started?
Did it come on suddenly or gradually?
Where exactly is the pain — can you point to it?
Does the pain change when you breathe in or cough?
Have you had anything like this before?
Have you ever been told you had a collapsed lung before?
Do you smoke — how much and for how long?
Do you have any lung conditions — asthma, COPD, or anything else?
Have you had any recent chest trauma or injury?
Any recent medical procedures or surgery?
Do you have any connective tissue conditions — Marfan syndrome or similar?
Any family history of collapsed lung?
Any leg swelling or calf pain?
Any fever or cough with phlegm?
